w55j5-8AAAAJ.keywords: Compilers, Embedded Systems, Hardware-assisted Security, Software Debloating, Memory Leak Detection, Program Analysis, Parallel and Distributed Systems, Compiler Optimizations, Intrusion and Anomaly Detection, Low-power Architecture
w55j5-8AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Santosh Pande is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group working at the intersection of **compilers**, **embedded systems**, and **system security**. His work combines *compiler techniques*, *hardware support*, and *runtime systems* to address problems in <u>information leakage</u>, <u>resource-efficient embedded execution</u>, and <u>software hardening and debloating</u>. Over a multi-decade career he has produced influential systems and compiler research that span from low-level processor-aware optimizations to practical security tools for production software.

---

## Research Areas
Pande’s research focuses on applied compiler and systems problems with a strong emphasis on security and constrained (embedded or many-core) environments. He has explored <u>information leakage</u> and tamper-resistant execution in hardware/software co-designed systems (e.g., "HIDE: an infrastructure for efficiently protecting information leakage on the address bus"), and developed *hardware-assisted* approaches to protect control flow on constrained devices ("Hardware assisted control flow obfuscation for embedded processors"). In the embedded and DSP space he has advanced compiler-driven storage assignment and code generation techniques ("Storage assignment optimizations to generate compact and efficient code on embedded DSPs", "Storage assignment optimizations through variable coalescence for embedded processors"), as well as power-aware scheduling and functional-unit gating ("Optimizing static power dissipation by functional units in superscalar processors"). Pande’s group has also produced practical tools and evaluations for software reliability and security—ranging from automated memory-leak detection ("Automated memory leak detection for production use", "Detecting memory leaks through introspective dynamic behavior modelling using machine learning") to software debloating and attack-surface reduction ("Blankit library debloating: Getting what you want instead of cutting what you don’t", "Carve: Practical security-focused software debloating using simple feature set mappings"). Complementing these topics are contributions to parallel and distributed compilation and runtime strategies ("Compilation techniques for parallel systems", "Case: A compiler-assisted scheduling framework for multi-gpu systems", and works on work-stealing and scheduling for multi-core/HPC).

---

## Notable Works
- "HIDE: an infrastructure for efficiently protecting information leakage on the address bus" (2004) — a seminal hardware/software co-design that exposed practical information-leakage channels on the address bus and proposed infrastructure to mitigate them, helping to shape thinking about microarchitectural side channels and tamper-resistant execution.
- "Brainy: Effective selection of data structures" (2011) — a systems-driven compiler/tooling contribution that analyzes data-structure behavior on real architectures to recommend better choices, demonstrating significant performance gains via architecture-aware data-structure selection.
- "Blankit library debloating: Getting what you want instead of cutting what you don’t" (2020) — a modern, security-focused effort addressing software bloat and code-reuse attack surfaces with practical debloating strategies for C/C++ libraries, influencing subsequent work on measurable security gains from debloating.

---

## Academic Background
Santosh Pande has been an active researcher since the mid-1990s and is a long-standing faculty member in the School of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where he directs a research group that spans graduate students and postdoctoral researchers. His publication record reflects sustained contributions across **compilers**, **embedded systems**, **security**, and **parallel systems**, accumulating over 2,400 citations, which attests to both depth and breadth of impact. Pande’s work appears consistently in top conferences and journals across systems, security, and compiler communities, and he has collaborated broadly with academic and industrial partners on topics ranging from low-level hardware-assisted defenses to production-quality software analysis tools. As a senior faculty member, he has played roles in mentoring Ph.D. students, shaping research agendas in systems security and compilers, and serving the community through program committees and editorial activities.
